content.text: Every token in an LLM prompt is processed by the model, which costs both time and money. When you send the same large system prompt — with thousands of tokens of instructions, examples, or retrieved context — on every API call, you pay for it in full each time. Prompt caching lets the provider retain the KV-cache state of the shared prefix and reuse it across requests, so you only pay to process those tokens once.

attrs.alt: Prompt caching comparison: three requests without caching bill the system prompt in full each time; with caching, requests 2 and 3 get a ~10% rate on the cached prefix. Shows Anthropic explicit cache_control and OpenAI automatic caching API patterns.

content.text: How Caching Works

When a request arrives, the provider checks whether the leading tokens match a cached prefix. If they do, the attention key-value tensors for those positions are already in GPU memory — the model only computes attention for the new (uncached) suffix. From the API perspective, cached tokens appear in
usage
as a separate counter and are billed at a discounted rate.
Anthropic
: you mark explicit cache breakpoints with
"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
. Cached tokens cost ~10% of the normal input rate. Cache entries expire after 5 minutes of disuse. Minimum cacheable block: 1024 tokens.
OpenAI

: caching is fully automatic for prompts whose prefix exceeds 1024 tokens. No special parameters needed. Cached tokens receive a 50% discount. The cache is keyed on the exact text of the prefix, so even a single character change invalidates it.
Anthropic Prompt Caching
Mark the end of the section you want cached with
cache_control
. Everything up to and including that block is the cached prefix. Everything after it (and the new user message) is processed fresh each turn.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

LONG_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a senior code reviewer...
[... 4000+ tokens of detailed instructions and examples ...]
"""

def review_code(user_code: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": LONG_SYSTEM_PROMPT,
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},  # cache this block
            }
        ],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Review:\n{user_code}"}],
    )

    usage = response.usage
    print(f"Input tokens:   {usage.input_tokens}")
    print(f"Cache write:    {usage.cache_creation_input_tokens}")
    print(f"Cache read:     {usage.cache_read_input_tokens}")
    # On first call: cache_creation_input_tokens ≈ 4000, cache_read = 0
    # On subsequent calls: cache_read ≈ 4000, cache_creation = 0

    return response.content[0].text
You can place up to four
cache_control

breakpoints in a single request. This is useful for caching progressively: a fixed system prompt, a set of retrieved documents, and a multi-turn conversation history can each be their own cached layer.
OpenAI Automatic Caching
OpenAI caches the longest matching prefix automatically. No API changes are needed — just ensure your high-token-count content comes first in the message list and remains stable across requests.

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = "..."  # ≥ 1024 tokens for caching to engage

def ask(user_message: str) -> str:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
            {"role": "user", "content": user_message},
        ],
    )

    details = response.usage.prompt_tokens_details
    print(f"Total prompt tokens: {response.usage.prompt_tokens}")
    print(f"Cached tokens:       {details.cached_tokens}")
    # On a cache hit, cached_tokens ≈ len(SYSTEM_PROMPT tokens)
    # Cached tokens billed at 50% of normal input rate

    return response.choices[0].message.content
Cache key stability
: any change to the cached prefix — including whitespace — creates a cache miss. Put dynamic content (user messages, per-request IDs) after the stable prefix, never before it.
Multi-Turn Conversation Caching
In a chat session, each turn appends a new message while all previous turns remain constant. By marking the conversation history as cached, you avoid reprocessing the entire history on each turn.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class CachedChatSession:
    def __init__(self, system_prompt: str):
        self.system = [
            {"type": "text", "text": system_prompt,
             "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}}
        ]
        self.history: list[dict] = []

    def send(self, user_message: str) -> str:
        self.history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_message})

        # Mark everything except the last user message as cached.
        # Build messages list: older turns marked for caching.
        messages = []
        for i, msg in enumerate(self.history[:-1]):
            if i == len(self.history) - 2:  # last assistant turn
                content = msg["content"]
                if isinstance(content, str):
                    content = [{"type": "text", "text": content,
                                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}}]
                messages.append({"role": msg["role"], "content": content})
            else:
                messages.append(msg)
        messages.append(self.history[-1])  # new user message, not cached

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=self.system,
            messages=messages,
        )

        reply = response.content[0].text
        self.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
        return reply
When Caching Pays Off
Use case
Stable prefix
Savings potential
High-volume chatbot
System prompt
High — same prompt on every request
RAG over static documents
System + retrieved chunks
High — docs don't change per query
Long multi-turn chat
System + prior turns
Medium — history grows each turn
Few-shot classification
System + examples
High — examples are identical across calls
One-shot unique requests
None (all dynamic)
None — no shared prefix
Measuring Cache Effectiveness
Track cache hits in production to verify the feature is working. A cache hit rate below ~80% on stable prompts suggests a content-stability issue — something is changing in the prefix unexpectedly.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from collections import deque

@dataclass
class CacheStats:
    reads: int = 0
    writes: int = 0
    input_tokens: int = 0
    recent_hit_rates: deque = field(default_factory=lambda: deque(maxlen=100))

    def record(self, usage) -> None:
        # Works for both Anthropic and OpenAI usage objects
        cached = getattr(usage, "cache_read_input_tokens",
                 getattr(getattr(usage, "prompt_tokens_details", None),
                         "cached_tokens", 0) or 0)
        total_input = getattr(usage, "input_tokens",
                     getattr(usage, "prompt_tokens", 0))

        self.reads += cached
        self.input_tokens += total_input
        self.recent_hit_rates.append(1 if cached > 0 else 0)

    @property
    def hit_rate(self) -> float:
        if not self.recent_hit_rates:
            return 0.0
        return sum(self.recent_hit_rates) / len(self.recent_hit_rates)

    @property
    def tokens_saved(self) -> int:
        # Anthropic: cached tokens cost 10% → 90% savings on those tokens
        # OpenAI: cached tokens cost 50% → 50% savings on those tokens
        return self.reads  # compare to self.input_tokens for full picture

stats = CacheStats()

# After each API call:
stats.record(response.usage)
print(f"Cache hit rate: {stats.hit_rate:.1%}")
print(f"Tokens read from cache: {stats.reads:,}")
Common Pitfalls
Dynamic content in the prefix
. Including a timestamp, request ID, or per-user data inside the system prompt before the cache breakpoint causes every request to be a cache miss. Move dynamic data to the user message.
Cache expiry on Anthropic

. Cache entries expire 5 minutes after last access. If your request rate is low (e.g. one request per 10 minutes), the cache expires between calls and you always pay the write fee. The OpenAI cache expires after 1 hour by default.
Forgetting the minimum size
. Both providers require at least 1024 tokens in the cached prefix. Short system prompts won't be cached regardless of the markup.
Cache write cost

. Anthropic charges a cache write fee (≈25% extra on the first write per entry). At very low request volumes the write cost can exceed the savings. Caching is most beneficial when the same prefix is used at least 2–3 times within the cache TTL.
Shared cache is per-organization, not per-user

. Multiple users hitting the same system prompt share the same cache, which is ideal for cost savings. There is no per-session isolation of cached KV state — only the prefix is cached, not inference state.
Why Prompt Caching Matters

At scale, input token costs dominate the cost profile of LLM-powered applications. A customer-support chatbot with a 5,000-token system prompt serving 100,000 requests per day processes 500 million input tokens daily — just from the system prompt alone, before accounting for user messages. At $3/M input tokens, that is $1,500 per day or $547,500 per year spent on reprocessing identical text.

Prompt caching changes the economics fundamentally. With Anthropic's 90% cached-token discount, the same 500M daily tokens from the system prompt cost $150/day instead of $1,500 — a $450/day saving on a single prompt. For RAG applications where 2,000-10,000 tokens of retrieved context are prepended to every request, the savings are even larger because the retrieved chunks repeat across queries within a session.

Beyond cost, caching reduces latency. When the attention KV cache for the prefix is already in GPU memory, the model only needs to compute attention for the new suffix tokens. For a 5,000-token system prompt with a 100-token user message, the model processes 100 tokens instead of 5,100 on a cache hit — a theoretical 51x reduction in time-to-first-token for the prefix portion.
Benefits
Dramatic cost reduction on repeated prefixes.

Anthropic charges 10% of normal input rates for cached tokens; OpenAI charges 50%. For workloads with large, stable system prompts or retrieved context, this directly cuts the variable cost of AI features.
Lower time-to-first-token.
The model skips attention computation over the cached prefix, reducing the time before the first output token arrives. This is most noticeable for very long system prompts (10,000+ tokens).
Enables longer contexts at reasonable cost.
Without caching, using a 100,000-token document as context on every API call would be prohibitively expensive. With caching, the document is processed once and reused across all follow-up questions.
No change to output quality.

Caching affects only which tokens are computed in the current request; the model's output is identical to what it would produce without caching. The cache is a computational optimisation, not a different model.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Limitation
Detail
Impact
Cache write surcharge
Anthropic charges ~25% extra on first write
Breakeven only after 2–3 hits per entry within the TTL
Short TTL (Anthropic)
Cache entries expire 5 minutes after last access
Low-traffic applications see few hits; cache constantly expires
Minimum token threshold
Both providers require ≥1024 tokens to cache
Short system prompts cannot benefit from caching
Exact prefix match required
Any change — including whitespace — invalidates cache
Dynamic content in prefix causes constant cache misses
Shared cache, per-organisation
Cache is not isolated per session or user
Multiple users benefit from same cache hit — desirable for shared prompts
Best Practices
Put all static content before dynamic content.
System instructions, personas, few-shot examples, and retrieved documents that do not change per request should all come before the user's actual message. This is the cache-friendly ordering.
Never put dynamic data (timestamps, request IDs, user names) in the cached prefix.
Any token that changes between requests must appear after the cache breakpoint, in the user message or in an uncached system block.
Monitor cache hit rate in production.

A hit rate below 80% on a prompt you believe is stable usually means something is changing unexpectedly. Log the fully-rendered prompt at least occasionally to confirm the prefix is identical across calls.
Use multiple cache breakpoints for layered caching.
Anthropic supports up to four cache_control breakpoints per request. Use them to separately cache the system instructions, the retrieved documents, and the conversation history.
For Anthropic: keep request rate above one per 5 minutes to sustain the cache.
Cache entries expire 5 minutes after last access. If your application has bursty traffic (quiet overnight, peak during business hours), expect cache misses at the start of each active period.
Real-World Example: RAG Chatbot Cost Optimisation

A production RAG-based legal research assistant prepends 8,000 tokens of retrieved case law documents to every query. With 5,000 queries per day, prompt caching reduces the monthly input token cost from $36,000 to roughly $3,600 — a 90% reduction. The implementation wraps the retrieved documents in a cached system block and keeps the user's actual question in the user message.

import anthropic
from functools import lru_cache

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS = """You are a legal research assistant.
You help lawyers find relevant precedents and explain their significance.
Always cite the case name and year. Do not give legal advice.
"""  # ~50 tokens — too short alone; combined with docs it exceeds 1024


def retrieve_relevant_cases(query: str) -> str:
    """Return the top-3 most relevant case documents (simplified)."""
    # In production: embedding search over case law database
    return f"[Retrieved case documents for: {query}]\n" + ("Case law text..." * 200)  # ~4000 tokens


def answer_legal_question(user_query: str) -> dict:
    """Answer a legal question using cached context."""
    relevant_cases = retrieve_relevant_cases(user_query)

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=[
            # Block 1: Static instructions — cache these
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS,
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
            },
            # Block 2: Retrieved context — cache per-session (changes per query set)
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Relevant case law:\n\n{relevant_cases}",
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
            },
        ],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_query}],
    )

    usage = response.usage
    cost_estimate = (
        usage.input_tokens * 15e-6  # $15/M for input (Anthropic claude-opus-4-5)
        + usage.cache_creation_input_tokens * 18.75e-6  # +25% for cache write
        + usage.cache_read_input_tokens * 1.5e-6  # 10% for cache read
        + usage.output_tokens * 75e-6  # $75/M output
    )

    return {
        "answer": response.content[0].text,
        "tokens_read_from_cache": usage.cache_read_input_tokens,
        "tokens_written_to_cache": usage.cache_creation_input_tokens,
        "estimated_cost_usd": round(cost_estimate, 6),
    }


# Multi-turn session: system + docs cached after first message
result1 = answer_legal_question("What is the standard for negligence in product liability?")
print(f"Q1 cache write: {result1['tokens_written_to_cache']} tokens")

result2 = answer_legal_question("How does strict liability differ from negligence in this context?")
print(f"Q2 cache read: {result2['tokens_read_from_cache']} tokens")
print(f"Q2 cost: ${result2['estimated_cost_usd']:.6f} (vs ~$0.12+ without caching)")
Comparison with Alternatives
Technique
What it caches
Cost saving
Latency saving
Implementation
Prompt caching (KV cache)
Attention state for prefix tokens
High (90% Anthropic, 50% OpenAI)
Medium (still runs model)
1–2 lines of code
Response caching (exact match)
Complete response for identical prompt
100% (free on hit)
Maximum (no model call)
External cache layer (Redis)
Semantic caching
Response for semantically similar prompt
High (for FAQ-style queries)
Maximum (no model call)
Embedding search + vector DB
Batch inference
Amortises model loading overhead
50% (Anthropic batch API)
Negative (24h delivery)
Async, non-real-time only
Performance and Scalability

The economics of prompt caching scale superlinearly with prefix length. A 1,000-token prefix saves fewer tokens per request than a 10,000-token prefix, and the savings per saved token are the same. The ROI of investing in longer, more detailed system prompts — which improve quality — increases when those tokens are cached rather than billed at full rate.

"""Cache ROI calculator for prompt caching."""

def cache_roi(
    prefix_tokens: int,
    requests_per_day: int,
    cache_hit_rate: float = 0.9,
    provider: str = "anthropic",
    input_price_per_m: float = 3.0,   # $/M tokens (e.g. claude-haiku)
) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate daily cost savings from prompt caching.

    Anthropic: cache write = 1.25x, cache read = 0.1x
    OpenAI:    cache read = 0.5x (no write surcharge)
    """
    hits = int(requests_per_day * cache_hit_rate)
    misses = requests_per_day - hits

    price = input_price_per_m / 1_000_000

    if provider == "anthropic":
        # First request: write fee; subsequent hits: 10% rate
        cost_without = requests_per_day * prefix_tokens * price
        # First miss writes the cache (1.25x); hits pay 0.1x
        cost_with = (
            misses * prefix_tokens * price * 1.25  # cache write on each miss
            + hits * prefix_tokens * price * 0.10  # cache read
        )
    else:  # openai
        cost_without = requests_per_day * prefix_tokens * price
        cost_with = (
            misses * prefix_tokens * price         # full rate on misses
            + hits * prefix_tokens * price * 0.50  # 50% on hits
        )

    savings = cost_without - cost_with
    return {
        "daily_cost_without_cache": round(cost_without, 4),
        "daily_cost_with_cache": round(cost_with, 4),
        "daily_savings": round(savings, 4),
        "monthly_savings": round(savings * 30, 2),
        "savings_pct": round(savings / cost_without * 100, 1),
    }

# Example: 5000-token system prompt, 10k requests/day, Anthropic
result = cache_roi(5000, 10_000, provider="anthropic")
print(f"Daily savings:   ${result['daily_savings']}")
print(f"Monthly savings: ${result['monthly_savings']}")
print(f"Savings: {result['savings_pct']}%")
# Output: Monthly savings: ~$391.50 (87% reduction)
Security and Reliability

Prompt caching is a shared resource: the same cached prefix can be used by any request in the same organisation that sends an identical prefix. This is by design and is the source of the cost savings. However, it means the cached prefix is not user-isolated — if your system prompt contains per-user customisation, that content must not appear in the cached prefix.
Never cache user-specific content.
User IDs, names, roles, or personalisation data must appear after the cache breakpoint in the user message, not in the cached system block. The cached prefix is shared across the organisation.
Never cache secrets or credentials.

If your system prompt contains API keys or internal URLs, those are sent to the provider and could be retained in logs. Use environment variables and inject secrets only at runtime, never in the static system prompt.
Cache-key stability is a correctness requirement.

If your cache prefix changes unexpectedly between releases (e.g., due to a template update that shifts a word), the cache invalidates silently and costs spike. Pin the prompt in version control and alert on cost anomalies.
Monitor cache hit rate as a health signal.

A sudden drop in cache hit rate indicates something changed in the prompt pipeline — either intentionally (a deployment) or unintentionally (a bug). Alert on hit rates below 70% for high-traffic prompts.
Key Takeaways
Caching reuses the attention KV state for the prefix.
The model skips recomputing attention for cached tokens, reducing both cost and latency.
Anthropic requires explicit cache_control markers; OpenAI caches automatically.
Both require ≥1024 tokens in the cached prefix. Anthropic: 90% discount, 5-min TTL, write surcharge. OpenAI: 50% discount, 1h TTL, no write surcharge.
Put static content first, dynamic content last.
Any token that changes between requests invalidates the cache for everything that follows it. System instructions, retrieved docs, and conversation history all come before the new user message.
ROI is highest for large prefixes at high request volume.
A 1,000-token system prompt at 10 requests/day barely benefits. A 10,000-token RAG context at 10,000 requests/day saves thousands of dollars per month.
Monitor cache hit rate in production.
A drop in hit rate signals an unexpected change in the prompt pipeline. Alert on anomalies and log fully-rendered prompts occasionally to confirm prefix stability.
Never cache user-specific or sensitive data.
The cached prefix is shared across the organisation. Personalisation, user IDs, and credentials must stay in the per-request user message, never in the cached prefix.
